title: Navigation Without GPS
summary: Finding direction and navigating without electronic devices
body:
## When Electronics Fail

GPS devices run out of batteries, break, or lose signal under heavy canopy. Knowing how to find direction and navigate with natural cues is a critical survival skill that requires no equipment.

## Sun-Based Navigation

### Shadow Stick Method

This reliable technique gives you an east-west line anywhere on Earth.

1. Place a straight stick (about 1m / 3ft) upright in the ground on a flat, sunny area
2. Mark the tip of the shadow with a small rock or scratch in the dirt
3. Wait **15-20 minutes**
4. Mark the new shadow tip position
5. Draw a line connecting the two marks - **this line runs approximately east-west**
6. The first mark is west, the second mark is east (shadows move opposite to the sun)
7. Stand with west to your left and east to your right - you are facing north (in the Northern Hemisphere)

**Note**: This method is most accurate around midday and at times other than close to the summer solstice at high latitudes.

### Watch Method (Northern Hemisphere)

- Hold an analog watch flat. Point the **hour hand** directly at the sun.
- Find the midpoint between the hour hand and 12 o'clock on the watch face.
- That midpoint direction is approximately **south**.
- During daylight saving time, use 1 o'clock instead of 12.

In the **Southern Hemisphere**, point the 12 at the sun, and the midpoint between 12 and the hour hand points **north**.

## Star Navigation

### Finding North (Northern Hemisphere)

- Locate the **Big Dipper** (Ursa Major) - seven bright stars forming a ladle shape
- Find the two stars at the outer edge of the "cup" (the pointer stars)
- Extend an imaginary line through these two stars about 5x the distance between them
- This line points to **Polaris (the North Star)**, which sits within 1 degree of true north
- Polaris is the last star in the handle of the Little Dipper (Ursa Minor)

**Tip**: Polaris is not the brightest star in the sky. It is moderately bright. If you find a very bright star, it is probably not Polaris.

### Finding South (Southern Hemisphere)

- Locate the **Southern Cross** (Crux) - four bright stars in a cross shape
- Extend the long axis of the cross 4.5x its length toward the horizon
- That point on the horizon is approximately **due south**
- You can also use the two pointer stars (Alpha and Beta Centauri) nearby to confirm

## Terrain Association

When you cannot see the sky, use the landscape itself:

- **Follow drainages downhill** - Streams lead to rivers, rivers lead to civilization in most regions
- **Ridgelines for visibility** - Travel along ridges when you need to see landmarks and maintain direction
- **Vegetation clues** - In the Northern Hemisphere, moss tends to grow thicker on the north side of trees (the shaded side), but this is only a rough indicator and unreliable as a sole method
- **Wind patterns** - If you know the prevailing wind direction in your area, leaning trees and flagged branches can confirm your heading

## Deliberate Offset Navigation

When navigating to a feature on a linear landmark (a bridge on a river, a cabin on a road):

- **Aim deliberately to one side** of your target by 10-15 degrees
- When you reach the linear feature, you will **know which direction to turn** to find your target
- If you aim straight at it and miss, you will not know whether to turn left or right

This technique prevents the most common navigation error in the field.

## Pace Counting

Pace counting lets you estimate distance traveled on foot.

- A **double pace** is counted each time your same foot hits the ground
- On flat terrain, the average person covers about **65 double paces per 100m (330ft)**
- Use a **pace count beads** system (ranger beads): slide one bead for every 100m, and one upper bead for every 1000m
- **Adjust for terrain**: Add roughly 10% more paces going uphill, through brush, or in sand and snow

## Dead Reckoning

Combine direction and distance to navigate without landmarks:

1. Determine your heading using any method above
2. Pick a visible landmark on that heading and walk to it
3. Count paces as you go
4. At the landmark, re-sight your heading and pick a new landmark
5. Record direction changes and distances (scratch in dirt, knots in cord)

**WARNING: Dead reckoning accumulates error over distance. Correct your position against known features whenever possible.**
